chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The group shifted from procedural introductions into generating and exploring specific research ideas, introducing new technologies and modeling approaches to address surveillance challenges.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Multiple non-speaking participants displayed consistent head nods and visible smiles while others presented their ideas, indicating active listening.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Connected pan-viral serology (VirScan) to ecological surveillance modeling.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: The discussion narrowed to the domain of optimizing wildlife surveillance using serology data, but a specific research question has not yet been articulated.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group identified a shared interest in combining surveillance modeling with novel serology data, but has not yet defined a specific project.
chunk_summary.shared_vision_quote: what I want to get out of this is like okay we have surveillance data we feed it into an automated modeling system
chunk_summary.funding_reference_description: Mentioned agency people looking for help detecting SARS-CoV-2 in animal reservoirs.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: The group successfully transitioned from individual introductions to joint ideation, with Efrem immediately identifying a technological solution (VirScan) that directly addressed the data limitations Kim raised for her modeling approach.

ideas_currently_on_table: Optimizing surveillance using serology data and automated modeling
Using VirScan/PhIP-Seq for pan-viral serology in wildlife
chunk_summaries: The group spent the segment establishing meeting logistics and conducting a structured round of personal introductions. Participants shared their diverse backgrounds in epidemiology, virology, engineering, and modeling, revealing a strong shared focus on disease surveillance. The tone was collaborative and organized, ending with the group still completing introductions and not yet generating specific project ideas.

The group transitioned from introductions to brainstorming, driven by Christine who proposed focusing on a few key questions. Kim proposed an idea about optimizing surveillance by feeding serology data into an automated modeling system to direct sampling efforts. Efrem built on this by introducing VirScan, a pan-viral serology technology, which Kim agreed could be valuable for broader surveillance after initial targeted efforts. The tone was engaged and collaborative, ending with the group actively exploring how to combine modeling and novel serology techniques.
